content:
# Philippians Chapter 4 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Philippians Chapter 4 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 4:1 -- "So-then" vs "Therefore" vs "So then"; "brothers" vs "brothers and sisters"; sentence structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "So-then, my brothers, beloved and longed-for, my joy and crown, thus stand-firm in [the] Lord, beloved-ones." | Hyper-literal, preserving Greek word order and hyphenated compounds. Uses "beloved-ones" at the end. |
| LOGOS | "Therefore, my brothers, beloved and longed for, my joy and crown, stand firm thus in the Lord, beloved." | Formal equivalent with natural English word order. "Therefore" for WSTE. |
| PNEUMA | "So then, my brothers and sisters — you whom I love and long for, my joy and my crown — stand firm in the Lord, my dear ones. This is how you must stand." | Adds "and sisters," restructures with dashes, adds "This is how you must stand" as an expansion, "my dear ones" for AGAPHTOI. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Therefore" is a strong formal connector for WSTE. "Brothers" follows the manuscript reading ADELFOI without gender-inclusive expansion. PNEUMA's added sentence "This is how you must stand" has no Greek basis. "Beloved" at the close is preferred over PNEUMA's "my dear ones" as more formally faithful.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:2 -- "I-exhort" vs "I urge" vs "I urge"; "the same [thing] to-think" vs "to be of the same mind" vs "to come to agreement"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Euodia I-exhort and Syntyche I-exhort the same [thing] to-think in [the] Lord." | Follows Greek word order (names before verb). "Exhort" for PARAKALW. "The same thing to-think" for TO AUTO FRONEIN. |
| LOGOS | "I urge Euodia, and I urge Syntyche, to be of the same mind in the Lord." | Natural English word order. "Urge" for PARAKALW. "To be of the same mind" for TO AUTO FRONEIN. |
| PNEUMA | "I urge Euodia and I urge Syntyche to come to agreement in the Lord." | "Come to agreement" is an interpretive rendering of TO AUTO FRONEIN, implying conflict resolution. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "I urge" is more natural than "I-exhort" and both are valid for PARAKALW. "To be of the same mind" is closer to the Greek TO AUTO FRONEIN (literally "to think the same thing") than PNEUMA's "to come to agreement," which implies a process not specified in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:3 -- "yokefellow" vs "companion" vs "partner"; "contended-together" vs "labored" vs "fought side by side"; "assembly" vs "church" vs implicit; structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Yes, I-ask also you, genuine yokefellow, assist them, who in the good-news contended-together with me, with also Clement and the rest of my fellow-workers, whose names [are] in [the] book of life." | "Yokefellow" preserves the etymological force of SUZUGE. "Contended-together" preserves the athletic metaphor of SUNHQLHSAN. "Good-news" for EUAGGELION. |
| LOGOS | "Yes, I ask you also, true companion, assist these women who labored with me in the gospel, together with Clement and the rest of my fellow workers, whose names are in the book of life." | "True companion" for GNHSIE SUZUGE. "Labored" flattens the athletic metaphor. "These women" is an interpretive addition specifying gender. |
| PNEUMA | "Yes, and I ask you too, my true partner, help these women. They fought side by side with me for the gospel, along with Clement and my other co-workers, whose names are written in the book of life." | "My true partner" adds possessive. "Fought side by side" captures the athletic/combat metaphor. "Help" for SULLAMBANOU. Breaks into two sentences. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus blends elements. "True companion" from LOGOS (balancing EMET's "yokefellow" which is too archaic for consensus and PNEUMA's "partner" which is too modern). "Contended together" is adopted from EMET rather than LOGOS's "labored," as SUNHQLHSAN is specifically an athletic metaphor that "labored" flattens. "These women" from LOGOS is retained as a clarifying addition consistent with the context (Euodia and Syntyche are female names). "In the gospel" follows LOGOS and PNEUMA over EMET's "good-news."

**Status:** Resolved as a blend — LOGOS base with EMET's "contended together" and LOGOS's "these women."

---

## 4:4 -- Punctuation and emphasis

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Rejoice in [the] Lord always; again I-will-say, rejoice." | Semicolon separates clauses. Period at end. Hyphenated "I-will-say." |
| LOGOS | "Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice." | Semicolon separates clauses. Period at end. |
| PNEUMA | "Rejoice in the Lord always! I will say it again: Rejoice!" | Exclamation marks. "I will say it again" adds "it." Colon before second "Rejoice." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's wording with an exclamation mark after the final "rejoice" to capture the emphatic force of the repetition. PNEUMA's "I will say it again" adds the pronoun "it" not in the Greek. The exclamation is warranted by the rhetorical intensity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with minor punctuation from PNEUMA's approach.

---

## 4:5 -- "men" vs "people" vs "everyone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let your gentleness be-known to all men. The Lord [is] near." | "Men" for ANQRWPOIS. Hyphenated "be-known." |
| LOGOS | "Let your gentleness be known to all people. The Lord is near." | "People" for ANQRWPOIS, gender-neutral rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "Let your gentleness be evident to everyone. The Lord is near." | "Evident" for GNWSQHTW. "Everyone" for PASIN ANQRWPOIS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "People" is a natural and accurate rendering of ANQRWPOIS in modern English. PNEUMA's "evident" is a valid alternative for GNWSQHTW but "be known" is closer to the Greek verb. "Everyone" collapses the two-word phrase PASIN ANQRWPOIS into one word.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:6 -- "Be-anxious-about nothing" vs "Be anxious about nothing" vs "Do not be anxious about anything"; structure of second clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Be-anxious-about nothing, but in everything by the prayer and the supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be-made-known before God." | Preserves the articles ("the prayer and the supplication"). "Before God" for PROS TON QEON. |
| LOGOS | "Be anxious about nothing, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God." | Drops articles before "prayer" and "supplication" for natural English. "To God" for PROS TON QEON. |
| PNEUMA | "Do not be anxious about anything. Instead, in every situation, bring your requests to God through prayer and petition, with thanksgiving." | Restructures into two sentences. "Bring your requests to God" reverses the word order. "Petition" for DEHSEI. "In every situation" for EN PANTI. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. It preserves the single-sentence structure of the Greek while reading naturally. PNEUMA's restructuring into two sentences and reordering of the elements departs from the Greek syntax. EMET's retention of the articles ("the prayer and the supplication") is technically accurate but awkward in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:7 -- "mind" vs "understanding" vs "human understanding"; "shall-guard" vs "shall guard" vs "will stand guard over"; "thoughts" vs "minds" vs "minds"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And the peace of God, the [one] surpassing every mind, shall-guard your hearts and your thoughts in Christ Jesus." | "Mind" for NOUN. "Thoughts" for NOHMATA. "The [one] surpassing" preserves the articular participle. |
| LOGOS | "And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, shall guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus." | "Understanding" for NOUN. "Minds" for NOHMATA. "Which surpasses" — relative clause. |
| PNEUMA | "And the peace of God, which surpasses all human understanding, will stand guard over your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus." | Adds "human" before "understanding." "Will stand guard over" expands the military metaphor. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Understanding" is the most widely attested translation of NOUN in this context and captures the cognitive faculty Paul intends. PNEUMA's "human" is an interpretive addition — while theologically implied, it is not in the Greek. "Shall guard" preserves the future indicative's promissory force better than "will stand guard over," which, while vivid, expands beyond the single verb FROURHSEI. "Minds" for NOHMATA is preferred over EMET's "thoughts" as NOHMATA encompasses the broader faculty of mind.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:8 -- "For-the-rest" vs "Finally" vs "Finally"; "righteous" vs "just" vs "right"; "well-spoken-of" vs "of good repute" vs "admirable"; "reckon" vs "think on" vs "dwell on"; "brothers" vs "brothers" vs "brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For-the-rest, brothers, whatever-things are true, whatever-things [are] honorable, whatever-things [are] righteous, whatever-things [are] pure, whatever-things [are] lovely, whatever-things [are] well-spoken-of, if [there is] any virtue and if any praise, these-things reckon;" | "For-the-rest" for TO LOIPON. "Righteous" for DIKAIA. "Well-spoken-of" for EUФHMA. "Reckon" for LOGIZESQE. |
| LOGOS | "Finally, brothers, whatever things are true, whatever things are honorable, whatever things are just, whatever things are pure, whatever things are lovely, whatever things are of good repute — if there is any virtue and if there is any praise — think on these things." | "Finally" for TO LOIPON. "Just" for DIKAIA. "Of good repute" for EUФHMA. "Think on" for LOGIZESQE. |
| PNEUMA | "Finally, brothers and sisters, fill your minds with whatever is true, whatever is worthy of respect, whatever is right, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is admirable — if anything is excellent or praiseworthy, dwell on these things." | Adds "and sisters." "Fill your minds with" expands LOGIZESQE. "Worthy of respect" for SEMNA. "Right" for DIKAIA. "Admirable" for EUФHMA. "Excellent" for ARETH. "Praiseworthy" for EPAINOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Finally" is standard for TO LOIPON. "Just" for DIKAIA is precise. "Of good repute" for EUФHMA captures the social dimension of the word. "Think on" for LOGIZESQE is more faithful than PNEUMA's "fill your minds with" (which adds a metaphor) or EMET's "reckon" (which sounds financial in modern English). PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" again adds to the manuscript reading.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:9 -- "which-things also you-learned" vs "What you have both learned" vs "Put into practice everything you learned"; sentence structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which-things also you-learned and received and heard and saw in me, these-things practice; and the God of peace shall-be with you." | Relative pronoun "which-things" opening. Greek word order preserved. |
| LOGOS | "What you have both learned and received and heard and seen in me, practice these things, and the God of peace shall be with you." | "What you have both learned" — natural English relative clause. Single sentence. |
| PNEUMA | "Put into practice everything you learned and received from me, everything you heard me say and saw me do. And the God of peace will be with you." | Imperative-first structure. "Everything you heard me say and saw me do" — significant expansion. Two sentences. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. It preserves the fourfold verb sequence (learned, received, heard, seen) in natural English while maintaining the single-sentence structure. PNEUMA's "everything you heard me say and saw me do" adds objects not in the Greek and restructures into two sentences.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:10 -- "you-revived the thinking on-behalf-of me" vs "you have revived your concern for me" vs "you have renewed your concern for me"; "you-were-lacking-opportunity" vs "you lacked opportunity" vs "you simply had no opportunity to show it"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But I-rejoiced in [the] Lord greatly, that now at-last you-revived the thinking on-behalf-of me, upon which also you-were-thinking, but you-were-lacking-opportunity." | "Revived the thinking on-behalf-of me" — hyper-literal for ANEQALETE TO UPER EMOU FRONEIN. "Upon which" preserves Greek relative construction. |
| LOGOS | "I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at length you have revived your concern for me, in which indeed you were concerned, but you lacked opportunity." | "At length" for HDE POTE. "Your concern for me" — smooth rendering of TO UPER EMOU FRONEIN. |
| PNEUMA | "I rejoice greatly in the Lord that at last you have renewed your concern for me. You were always concerned, of course — you simply had no opportunity to show it." | "Renewed" for ANEQALETE. "You were always concerned, of course" — interpretive addition of "always" and "of course." "No opportunity to show it" adds "to show it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with "at last" (from PNEUMA, matching EMET's "at-last") rather than "at length." "At last" better captures HDE POTE. PNEUMA's additions ("always," "of course," "to show it") are interpretive expansions. EMET's "the thinking on-behalf-of me" is too wooden.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with "at last" from EMET/PNEUMA.

---

## 4:11 -- "according-to lack" vs "according to want" vs "because I am in need"; "self-sufficient to-be" vs "to be content" vs "to be content"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Not that according-to lack I-speak, for I have-learned in whatever [circumstances] I-am, self-sufficient to-be." | "According-to lack" for KAQE USTERHSIN. "Self-sufficient" for AUTARKHS — Stoic philosophical term. |
| LOGOS | "Not that I speak according to want, for I have learned to be content in whatever circumstances I am." | "Want" for USTERHSIN. "Content" for AUTARKHS. |
| PNEUMA | "I am not saying this because I am in need. For I have learned to be content in whatever circumstances I find myself." | "I am not saying this because I am in need" — restructured paraphrase. "I find myself" adds a reflexive not in the Greek. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Want" for USTERHSIN is appropriate. "Content" is the standard English rendering of AUTARKHS, though EMET's "self-sufficient" captures the Stoic philosophical register — a nuance worth noting but too technical for consensus. PNEUMA restructures the sentence away from the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:12 -- "to-be-humbled" vs "to be brought low" vs "to have nothing"; "I-have-been-initiated" vs "I have been initiated" vs "I have been initiated into the secret"; structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-know also to-be-humbled, I-know also to-abound; in everything and in all-things I-have-been-initiated, both to-be-filled and to-hunger, both to-abound and to-lack." | "To-be-humbled" for TAPEINOUSQAI. "I-have-been-initiated" for MEMUHEMAI. Semicolon structure. |
| LOGOS | "I know how to be brought low, and I know how to abound. In everything and in all things I have been initiated — both to be filled and to hunger, both to abound and to be in want." | "To be brought low" for TAPEINOUSQAI. "I have been initiated" for MEMUHEMAI. Em-dash before the paired contrasts. |
| PNEUMA | "I know what it is to have nothing, and I know what it is to have plenty. I have been initiated into the secret of living in every possible situation — whether well-fed or hungry, whether in abundance or in want." | "To have nothing/to have plenty" — free paraphrase. "Initiated into the secret" adds "the secret." "Whether...or" restructures the paired contrasts. "Living in every possible situation" expands EN PANTI KAI EN PASIN. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "To be brought low" captures TAPEINOUSQAI better than PNEUMA's "to have nothing" (which loses the humiliation connotation) while being more natural than EMET's "to-be-humbled." "I have been initiated" without PNEUMA's addition of "into the secret" is more faithful — MEMUHEMAI is a standalone perfect passive and does not require an object to be supplied. LOGOS's structure with the em-dash effectively presents the paired contrasts.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:13 -- "I-am-strong-for all-things" vs "I can do all things" vs "I can face anything"; "empowering" vs "strengthens" vs "gives me strength"; absence of "Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-am-strong-for all-things in the [one] empowering me." | PANTA ISXUW rendered as "I-am-strong-for all-things." "Empowering" for ENDUNAMOUNTI. Correctly omits "Christ." |
| LOGOS | "I can do all things through the one who strengthens me." | "I can do all things" — traditional rendering of PANTA ISXUW. "Strengthens" for ENDUNAMOUNTI. "Through" for EN (instrumental). Correctly omits "Christ." |
| PNEUMA | "I can face anything through the one who gives me strength." | "I can face anything" — contextual rendering limiting scope to endurance. "Gives me strength" — periphrastic for ENDUNAMOUNTI. Correctly omits "Christ." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The consensus renders this as "I have strength for all things through the one who empowers me." This mediates between all three translations. "I have strength for all things" is closer to the Greek PANTA ISXUW (literally "I am strong for all things") than LOGOS's traditional "I can do all things" (which implies capability rather than strength) or PNEUMA's "I can face anything" (which narrows the scope to endurance). "Empowers" is adopted from EMET as the most precise rendering of ENDUNAMOUNTI, preserving the DUNAMIS root. "Through" follows LOGOS and PNEUMA for EN in its instrumental sense.

**Critical textual note:** All three translators correctly followed the Sinaiticus reading, which omits XRISTW ("Christ") after ENDUNAMOUNTI ME. The manuscript (page 197, column 2) clearly reads PANTA ISXUW EN TW ENDUNAMOUNTI ME without any addition. The familiar rendering "I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me" derives from later manuscripts and the Textus Receptus tradition. The shorter Sinaiticus reading is widely regarded by modern textual critics as more original; the addition of "Christ" is a typical scribal clarification. This is one of the most significant textual variants in the entire New Testament.

**Status:** Resolved as a mediating blend of all three, with prominent textual note.

---

## 4:14 -- "well you-did, having-shared-together with my affliction" vs "you did well in sharing with me in my affliction" vs "it was good of you to share in my troubles"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Nevertheless, well you-did, having-shared-together with my affliction." | "Having-shared-together" for SUGKOINWNHSANTES. "Affliction" for QLIYEI. Greek word order. |
| LOGOS | "Nevertheless, you did well in sharing with me in my affliction." | Natural English word order. "Sharing with me" for SUGKOINWNHSANTES. "Affliction" for QLIYEI. |
| PNEUMA | "Still, it was good of you to share in my troubles." | "Still" for PLHN. "It was good of you" — idiomatic paraphrase. "Troubles" for QLIYEI. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Nevertheless" is a stronger connector for PLHN than PNEUMA's "Still." "You did well in sharing with me in my affliction" preserves both the commendation and the participial structure. PNEUMA's "troubles" is too casual for QLIYEI, which carries connotations of tribulation and oppression.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:15 -- "good-news" vs "gospel" vs "gospel"; "assembly" vs "church" vs "church"; "account of giving and receiving" vs "matter of giving and receiving" vs "partnership of giving and receiving"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And you-know also, you Philippians, that in [the] beginning of the good-news, when I-went-out from Macedonia, no assembly shared-with me in [the] account of giving and receiving except you alone;" | "Good-news" for EUAGGELION. "Assembly" for EKKLHSIA. "Account" for LOGON. |
| LOGOS | "And you yourselves know, Philippians, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church shared with me in the matter of giving and receiving except you alone;" | "Gospel" for EUAGGELION. "Church" for EKKLHSIA. "Matter" for LOGON. |
| PNEUMA | "And you Philippians know that in the early days of the gospel, when I left Macedonia, no church entered into a partnership of giving and receiving with me — no one except you alone." | "Early days" for ARXH. "Entered into a partnership" — expanded rendering. "No one except you alone" — emphatic addition. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Gospel" and "church" are standard translations. "In the matter of giving and receiving" captures EIS LOGON DOSEWS KAI LHMYEWS (a commercial/accounting idiom) better than EMET's "account" (too financial) or PNEUMA's "partnership" (which is KOINWNIA, not LOGOS). PNEUMA's "early days" loosens ARXH ("beginning").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:16 -- "both once and twice" vs "once and again" vs "more than once"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because also in Thessalonica both once and twice you-sent to my need." | "Both once and twice" — literal rendering of KAI APAX KAI DIS. "Because" for OTI. |
| LOGOS | "for even in Thessalonica you sent to my need once and again." | "Once and again" — idiomatic English for KAI APAX KAI DIS. "For even" for OTI KAI. |
| PNEUMA | "For even while I was in Thessalonica, you sent me aid more than once when I was in need." | "While I was in Thessalonica" — adds temporal clause. "More than once" — interpretive summary. "When I was in need" — restructures EIS THN XREIAN. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a blend: "for even in Thessalonica you sent to my need both once and again." EMET's "both once and twice" preserves the Hebraic idiom KAI APAX KAI DIS most literally. LOGOS's "once and again" is the most natural English idiom. The consensus uses "both once and again" to preserve the KAI...KAI structure while remaining idiomatic. PNEUMA's "more than once" loses the specific idiom entirely, and "while I was in" and "when I was in need" are additions.

**Status:** Resolved as a blend of EMET and LOGOS.

---

## 4:17 -- "I-seek-after" vs "I seek" vs "I am looking for"; "the fruit, the [one] abounding" vs "the fruit that increases" vs "the profit that accumulates"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Not that I-seek-after the gift, but I-seek-after the fruit, the [one] abounding to your account." | "Seek-after" for EPIZHTW. "The fruit, the [one] abounding" preserves the articular participle. |
| LOGOS | "Not that I seek the gift, but I seek the fruit that increases to your account." | "Seek" for EPIZHTW. "That increases" for TON PLEONAZONTA. |
| PNEUMA | "Not that I am looking for a gift; what I am looking for is the profit that accumulates to your account." | "Looking for" — colloquial for EPIZHTW. "Profit" for KARPON — interpretive shift from agricultural to financial metaphor. "Accumulates" for PLEONAZONTA. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Seek" is appropriately formal for EPIZHTW. "Fruit" correctly renders KARPON (PNEUMA's "profit" changes the metaphor). "That increases" captures PLEONAZONTA well. The commercial language of "account" (LOGON) is preserved by all three.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:18 -- "I-have-received all-things and I-abound" vs "I have received all things in full and abound" vs "I have been paid in full and have more than enough"; "fragrance of sweet-smell" vs "fragrant offering" vs "fragrant offering"; "the [things] from you" vs "the things sent from you" vs "what you sent"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But I-have-received all-things and I-abound; I-have-been-filled, having-received from Epaphroditus the [things] from you, a fragrance of sweet-smell, a sacrifice acceptable, well-pleasing to God." | "Fragrance of sweet-smell" — literal double rendering of OSMHN EUWDIAS. "The [things] from you" — literal for TA PAR UMWN. |
| LOGOS | "But I have received all things in full and abound; I have been filled, having received from Epaphroditus the things sent from you, a fragrant offering, an acceptable sacrifice, well-pleasing to God." | "In full" added to APEXW (which implies receipt-in-full). "Fragrant offering" for OSMHN EUWDIAS. "The things sent from you" adds "sent." |
| PNEUMA | "I have been paid in full and have more than enough. I am fully supplied now that I have received from Epaphroditus what you sent — a fragrant offering, a sacrifice acceptable and pleasing to God." | "Paid in full" — commercial metaphor for APEXW. "Have more than enough" for PERISSOUW. "Fully supplied" for PEPLHRWMAI. Restructures into two sentences. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with a minor adjustment: "the things from you" (dropping "sent," which is not in the Greek, following EMET). LOGOS's "in full" correctly captures the commercial connotation of APEXW (a technical receipt term). "Fragrant offering" is a better consensus rendering than EMET's overly literal "fragrance of sweet-smell." PNEUMA's "paid in full" is accurate for APEXW but too commercial in register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with EMET's "the things from you."

---

## 4:19 -- "shall-fill-full" vs "shall supply" vs "will fully supply"; "your every need" vs "every need of yours" vs "every need of yours"; "his riches in glory" vs "his riches in glory" vs "his glorious riches"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And my God shall-fill-full your every need according-to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus." | "Shall-fill-full" for PLHRWSEI. "Your every need" — possessive before adjective. |
| LOGOS | "And my God shall supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus." | "Shall supply" for PLHRWSEI. "Every need of yours" — standard English order. |
| PNEUMA | "And my God will fully supply every need of yours according to his glorious riches in Christ Jesus." | "Will fully supply" for PLHRWSEI. "His glorious riches" restructures EN DOXH from prepositional phrase to adjective. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Shall supply" captures PLHRWSEI in its sense of filling/fulfilling needs. "According to his riches in glory" preserves the prepositional phrase EN DOXH as Paul wrote it, rather than PNEUMA's "glorious riches," which converts the prepositional phrase into an adjective and changes the meaning (the riches are "in glory," not merely "glorious"). This is a theologically significant distinction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:20 -- "to the ages of the ages" vs "forever and ever" vs "forever and ever"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And to our God and Father [be] the glory to the ages of the ages; amen." | "To the ages of the ages" — literal rendering of EIS TOUS AIWNAS TWN AIWNWN. Lowercase "amen." |
| LOGOS | "To our God and Father be the glory forever and ever. Amen." | "Forever and ever" — standard English idiom. Capitalized "Amen." |
| PNEUMA | "To our God and Father be glory forever and ever. Amen." | Drops article before "glory." Otherwise matches LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Forever and ever" is the standard English rendering of EIS TOUS AIWNAS TWN AIWNWN. The article before "glory" (TH DOXA) is preserved. PNEUMA's omission of "the" before "glory" is a minor inaccuracy. EMET's "to the ages of the ages" is too literal for consensus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:21 -- "holy-one" vs "saint" vs "one of God's people"; "brothers, the [ones] with me" vs "brothers who are with me" vs "brothers and sisters who are with me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Greet every holy-one in Christ Jesus. The brothers, the [ones] with me, greet you." | "Holy-one" for AGION. "The brothers, the [ones] with me" — preserves articular participle. |
| LOGOS | "Greet every saint in Christ Jesus. The brothers who are with me greet you." | "Saint" for AGION. Natural relative clause. |
| PNEUMA | "Greet every one of God's people in Christ Jesus. The brothers and sisters who are with me send their greetings." | "Every one of God's people" — periphrastic for PANTA AGION. "Brothers and sisters" — gender-inclusive expansion. "Send their greetings" — expansion of ASPAZONTAI. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Saint" is the standard English rendering of AGIOS in the Pauline epistles. PNEUMA's "every one of God's people" is a paraphrase. "Brothers" follows the manuscript. "Greet you" is more concise and faithful than PNEUMA's "send their greetings."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:22 -- "holy-ones" vs "saints" vs "God's people"; "the [ones] of the household of Caesar" vs "those of Caesar's household" vs "those who belong to Caesar's household"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All the holy-ones greet you, and especially the [ones] of the household of Caesar." | "Holy-ones" for AGIOI. "The [ones] of the household of Caesar" — literal with articular construction. |
| LOGOS | "All the saints greet you, and especially those of Caesar's household." | "Saints" for AGIOI. "Those of Caesar's household" — natural English possessive. |
| PNEUMA | "All of God's people here send you greetings, especially those who belong to Caesar's household." | "God's people" — paraphrase. "Here" — added. "Send you greetings" — expansion. "Who belong to" — added. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Saints" is consistent with v. 21. "Those of Caesar's household" is concise and natural. PNEUMA adds "here," "who belong to," and uses "send you greetings" instead of the simpler "greet you."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:23 -- Presence or absence of "Amen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ [be] with your spirit." | No "Amen." Brackets around supplied verb. |
| LOGOS | "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen." | Includes "Amen." |
| PNEUMA | "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit." | No "Amen." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the reading without "Amen," following EMET and PNEUMA. The Sinaiticus manuscript transitions from the benediction directly to the subscriptio PROS FILIPPHSIOUS without a clear "Amen" at the end of the letter. While LOGOS includes "Amen" (following some manuscript traditions and the "Amen" visible elsewhere), the more conservative reading omits it. The verb "be" is supplied without brackets for readability.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET and PNEUMA (no "Amen").

---

## Summary

Of the 23 verses in Philippians 4, significant divergences were found in all 23 verses. The disagreements ranged from minor stylistic differences (punctuation, article usage) to substantive interpretive choices (v. 13's rendering of PANTA ISXUW, v. 12's mystery-religion vocabulary, v. 19's prepositional phrase EN DOXH).

LOGOS served as the primary base for the consensus in the majority of verses, with EMET contributing key readings in vv. 3 ("contended together"), 16 ("both once and again"), and 18 ("the things from you"). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings, while vivid and communicatively effective, frequently added words or restructured sentences beyond what the Greek warrants for a consensus translation.

The most significant textual issue in this chapter is the absence of "Christ" in v. 13, faithfully followed by all three translators.

— SOPHIA
